step1_context.flow_summary: The Fourth Servant Song (Isaiah 52:13–53:12) portrays a mysterious figure despised and rejected by humanity. The passage moves from the Servant’s disfigurement and rejection (52:13–53:3), through the vicarious nature of his suffering (53:4–6), to his silent submission (53:7–9), and finally to his vindication and the fruit of his anguish (53:10–12). Verses 5–6 stand at the theological center: the Servant is pierced for the transgressions of others, crushed for their iniquities, and through his wounds healing comes. Verse 6 universalizes human guilt—all have gone astray like sheep—and declares that the LORD has laid on the Servant the iniquity of all.
step1_context.genre_rationale: Isaiah 53 is part of the Servant Songs, written in elevated Hebrew poetry within a prophetic oracle. It employs parallelism, figurative language, and prophetic vision.
step1_context.redemptive_historical_rationale: The Suffering Servant’s vicarious, substitutionary suffering anticipates the atoning death of Christ on the cross. The New Testament explicitly applies this passage to Jesus (Matthew 8:17; Acts 8:32–35; 1 Peter 2:24–25).

renderings.text: But he was wounded for our transgressions, he was bruised for our iniquities: the chastisement of our peace was upon him; and with his stripes we are healed. All we like sheep have gone astray; we have turned every one to his own way; and the LORD hath laid on him the iniquity of us all.

But he was pierced for our transgressions; he was crushed for our iniquities; upon him was the chastisement that brought us peace, and with his wounds we are healed. All we like sheep have gone astray; we have turned—every one—to his own way; and the LORD has laid on him the iniquity of us all.

But he was pierced for our transgressions, he was crushed for our iniquities; the punishment that brought us peace was on him, and by his wounds we are healed. We all, like sheep, have gone astray, each of us has turned to our own way; and the LORD has laid on him the iniquity of us all.

variant_signals.impact: KJV renders chalal as 'wounded' while ESV and NIV use 'pierced.' The latter more precisely reflects the violent, penetrating nature of the Hebrew verb and resonates with the New Testament piercing motif (John 19:34, Zechariah 12:10).

KJV and ESV retain 'chastisement' (musar), which carries overtones of corrective discipline, while NIV uses 'punishment,' emphasizing penal consequences. This distinction shapes whether the suffering is understood primarily as disciplinary or retributive.

KJV’s 'stripes' (chavvurah) evokes the image of lash marks, while ESV and NIV use 'wounds,' a broader term. 'Stripes' emphasizes the specific mode of suffering; 'wounds' generalizes the concept of injury that brings healing.

step2_translations.impact_summary: The major translation differences revolve around three Hebrew terms: chalal ('wounded'/'pierced'), musar ('chastisement'/'punishment'), and chavvurah ('stripes'/'wounds'). These variations affect the vividness and theological precision of the substitutionary atonement portrayed—whether the emphasis falls on penetrating violence, corrective discipline versus penal suffering, and specific lashing versus general injury. All translations preserve the core theology: the Servant suffers vicariously so that others are healed and made whole.

usage_examples.snippet: my heart is wounded (chalal) within me
Will you still say, 'I am a god,' in the presence of those who slay (chalal) you?

keywords.meaning_in_context: Fatally pierced or mortally wounded—describing the Servant’s violent, substitutionary death on behalf of transgressors

usage_examples.snippet: do not despise the LORD’s discipline (musar)

keywords.meaning_in_context: The penalty or corrective suffering borne by the Servant that secures peace (shalom) for others—combining the ideas of punishment due and the restoration it produces

usage_examples.snippet: I am the LORD who heals (rapha) you

keywords.meaning_in_context: Spiritual healing and restoration from the sickness of sin—the Servant’s wounds become the means by which broken humanity is made whole before God

usage_examples.snippet: a broken and crushed (daka) heart, O God, you will not despise
it was the will of the LORD to crush (daka) him

keywords.meaning_in_context: The Servant is utterly broken and shattered under the weight of the iniquities placed upon him—emphasizing the completeness of the suffering endured

usage_examples.snippet: I have gone astray (taah) like a lost sheep
My sheep wandered (taah) over all the mountains

keywords.meaning_in_context: Universal human rebellion pictured as sheep straying from the shepherd—every person has turned to his own autonomous path away from God

edges.snippet: He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree, that we might die to sin and live to righteousness. By his wounds you have been healed. For you were straying like sheep, but have now returned to the Shepherd.
He was delivered up for our trespasses and raised for our justification.
This was to fulfill what was spoken by the prophet Isaiah: 'He took our illnesses and bore our diseases.'
For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.
For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, and are justified by his grace as a gift, through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus, whom God put forward as a propitiation by his blood.
Yet it was the will of the LORD to crush him; he has put him to grief... he shall bear their iniquities... he bore the sin of many and makes intercession for the transgressors.
My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?... they have pierced my hands and feet... they divide my garments among them.

clusters.name: Vicarious Substitutionary Atonement
clusters.rationale: Passages that develop the theology of one bearing the penalty in the place of others, directly echoing Isaiah 53’s substitutionary framework

clusters.name: Universal Human Sinfulness
clusters.rationale: Passages paralleling the 'all we like sheep have gone astray' theme of universal guilt and the need for divine intervention

clusters.name: Healing Through Suffering
clusters.rationale: Passages connecting the Servant’s wounds and suffering with the healing, restoration, or redemption of God’s people

clusters.name: Messianic Suffering Prophecy
clusters.rationale: Old Testament passages that prophetically depict the suffering of God’s anointed one, forming a composite portrait fulfilled in Christ

clusters.name: New Testament Fulfillment Citations
clusters.rationale: Passages where New Testament authors explicitly cite or allude to Isaiah 53 as fulfilled in Jesus

principles.statement: Healing and peace with God come only through the Servant’s substitutionary suffering
principles.grounding: Verse 5 establishes a direct causal link: he was pierced 'for our transgressions,' crushed 'for our iniquities,' and 'by his wounds we are healed.' The suffering is not accidental but purposeful and vicarious.

principles.statement: Every human being shares in the universal condition of straying from God
principles.grounding: Verse 6 uses emphatic, all-encompassing language—'all we like sheep have gone astray; we have turned every one to his own way'—leaving no room for self-exemption from the diagnosis of sin.

principles.statement: God is the initiator of redemption, laying the cost on the Servant rather than on the guilty
principles.grounding: The climactic clause 'the LORD has laid on him the iniquity of us all' reveals divine initiative: God himself transfers the burden of guilt onto the willing Servant, demonstrating sovereign grace.

prompts.text: Do I truly believe that my peace with God rests entirely on what the Servant accomplished, not on my own moral effort? Where am I tempted to add my own works to his finished suffering?
Verse 6 says each of us has 'turned to his own way.' What specific area of autonomy or self-direction am I clinging to instead of following the Shepherd?
Lord, I confess that I have gone astray like a sheep, turning to my own way. Thank you for laying my iniquity on your Servant. Help me to live in the healing and peace his wounds have purchased.
Since all have gone astray equally, how does this passage reshape the way our community treats those whose sin is more visible? How can we embody the grace modeled here?
What does it mean that 'the LORD has laid on him the iniquity of us all'? How does God’s sovereign initiative in atonement guard against both despair and presumption?
